worth_it_explanation: Access to the OpenX publisher console controls who can change floors, see revenue, manage demand partners, and pull credentials, so the people and tokens that hold that access are part of the monetization setup. A console where admin rights are wider than the work requires, where departed staff still have logins, or where an API credential is shared and never rotated, widens the blast radius of any mistake or compromise without showing up in a revenue report. The check is a review of the access list a publisher already manages, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to specific accounts and credentials to tighten.
intro: Access controls decide who can act inside the OpenX publisher console and what each person or integration can reach. This subcategory is about the access list itself, the human users who sign in and the API credentials that systems use, rather than the pricing or inventory those users configure. These checks confirm that access follows the principle of least privilege, that it tracks the current team rather than a list that only ever grows, and that programmatic credentials are scoped and rotated rather than shared and left in place.

audit_checks.title: Console roles do not follow least privilege and departed users still have access
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Access to the OpenX console carries the power to change floors, read revenue, and manage demand connections, so every account with more reach than its work requires widens the blast radius of a mistake or a compromised login. A departed employee whose access was never removed is a standing entry point that no current person controls, which makes the access list a security gap rather than a reflection of the team.

educate.base:
The OpenX publisher console is where a publisher manages the levers that drive monetization, including floor pricing, inventory setup, demand connections, and revenue reporting. Access to that console is granted to human users through roles, and each role groups the capabilities a person can use. The principle of least privilege holds that a person should have only the access the work requires, no more, so that a mistake or a compromised login can reach only a narrow part of the account rather than all of it.

Two patterns work against that principle. The first is over-broad access, where accounts carry admin or full-edit rights that the day-to-day work does not call for. An analyst who only reads reports does not need the rights to change floors, and a contractor working on one section does not need reach across the whole account. The second is access that outlives the person. When an employee leaves or a contractor's engagement ends and the login is never removed, that account becomes a standing entry point that no current person controls, which is a security exposure independent of whether anyone misuses it.

The fix for both is a periodic access review. A publisher lists every active user, confirms the role each holds is the narrowest that fits the work, and removes any account tied to a person who has left. Industry security guidance frames this as routine hygiene. The Amazon Web Services (AWS) identity guidance, as one widely cited example, recommends granting least-privilege permissions and regularly reviewing and removing access that is no longer needed, and the same principle applies to any console that holds sensitive controls.

A few signs point to access that has drifted from least privilege:
  • Several accounts hold admin or full-edit rights when their work only calls for read access or a narrow section
  • The active-user list includes names of people who have left the company or finished a contract
  • No record exists of when access was last reviewed, or who owns the review
You may want to confirm that each account's role is the narrowest that fits its work and that the active-user list matches the current team. One approach is to run a scheduled access review, on a quarterly cadence, that checks each role against the work and removes any login tied to a departed person.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, an editorial team may have several people who need to read revenue or inventory reports but only a few who should change floors or demand settings. You may want to separate read-only reporting access from the edit rights that move pricing, so the larger group can see the numbers without holding the power to change the configuration behind them.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the people managing ad monetization often sit alongside a separate team handling storefront and analytics, and access can blur between them. You may want to confirm that console rights are scoped to the people doing ad-monetization work specifically, rather than extended to a broader group whose work does not touch the ad setup.

learn_more.source_type: AWS Identity and Access Management documentation
learn_more.source_title: Security best practices in IAM (least privilege, regularly review and remove unused access)

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed configuration documentation for managing console users and roles is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the list of active console users, the role each holds, and whether any account belongs to a person who has left, to see whether access follows least privilege and tracks the current team.

openx_console.label: OpenX publisher console
steps: Open the account or user-management section of the OpenX console and list every active user with the role assigned to each.
For each user, note whether the role is admin, full-edit, or read-only, and compare that level against the work the person actually does.
Flag any account holding admin or full-edit rights whose work would be served by a narrower role.
Cross-check the active-user list against the current team roster and flag any login tied to a person who has left or whose contract has ended.
Ask the publisher when access was last reviewed and who owns that review, and record the answer.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active console user, the role held, the work that role supports, and whether the account belongs to a current team member. An access list where every role is the narrowest that fits the work and every login maps to a current person passes. An access list with admin or full-edit rights beyond the work, or with logins tied to departed people, is a fail recorded with the specific accounts and the gap each represents.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active user, the role held, and whether the account belongs to a current team member above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that access to {{site_name}}'s OpenX console does not follow least privilege, with roles broader than the work requires and, in some cases, logins still active for people who have left. Every account with more reach than its work calls for widens the blast radius of a mistake or a compromised login, and a departed person's standing login is an entry point no current person controls. {{voice_recommend}} running a scheduled access review that narrows each role to the work it supports, removes any login tied to a departed person, and sets an owner and a cadence so the access list keeps tracking the current team. After the review, confirm that each remaining account holds only the rights its work requires.

audit_checks.title: API credentials are shared, broadly scoped, and never rotated
audit_checks.impact_rationale: API credentials that reach the OpenX account programmatically can read revenue and change configuration without anyone signing in, so a credential that is shared across people or systems, scoped beyond what its integration needs, or never rotated turns one leak into broad, untraceable access. A shared key also breaks accountability because an action taken with it cannot be tied back to a single person or system.

educate.base:
Beyond the people who sign in, the OpenX account can be reached programmatically through the OpenX Application Programming Interface (API), which uses credentials such as a consumer key and consumer secret to authenticate a system rather than a person. OpenX's own public client libraries document this OAuth flow, including the OX3 PHP API client and the newer Python API client, both of which instantiate with a key and secret to log in and carry that authentication on every request. Those credentials reach the same controls a console login does, so they call for the same discipline around scope, ownership, and rotation.

Three habits weaken that discipline. The first is sharing a single credential across several people or several systems, which means an action taken with the key cannot be traced back to one person or one integration, and revoking it to contain a problem breaks everyone at once. The second is over-broad scope, where a credential built for one task, for example pulling a nightly report, holds rights to change configuration it never needs. The third is leaving a credential in place indefinitely. A key that is never rotated stays valid long after the person who created it has moved on, and any copy that leaked at any point remains usable.

The safer pattern gives each integration its own credential, scoped to the narrowest set of rights that task requires, owned by a named person or system, and rotated on a regular schedule. The same identity guidance that covers human access extends to programmatic keys. The Amazon Web Services (AWS) identity documentation, again as a widely cited example, recommends updating long-term credentials such as access keys when needed, for example when an employee leaves, and applying least privilege to what each credential can reach.

A few signs point to API credentials that need attention:
  • One credential is shared across multiple people or multiple integrations, so its actions cannot be traced to a single source
  • A credential carries broad rights when its integration only needs to read a report or touch one part of the account
  • No credential has been rotated in a long stretch, and no schedule or owner exists for rotation
You may want to confirm that each integration holds its own scoped credential with a named owner and a rotation schedule. One approach is to inventory the active API credentials, map each to the integration and owner it belongs to, narrow any that are over-scoped, and set a rotation cadence.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, automated reporting that feeds a revenue dashboard or a data warehouse often runs on an API credential, and that pull usually needs only read access. You may want to confirm the reporting credential is scoped to read-only rather than carrying configuration rights it never uses, and that it is separate from any credential used to change settings.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, ad-monetization data is sometimes pulled into the same analytics stack that tracks store performance, which can lead to one shared credential spanning several systems. You may want to give each system its own scoped credential with a named owner, so a problem in one integration can be contained without revoking access for all of them.

learn_more.source_title: OX3 PHP API Client (OAuth login, consumer key and secret)
OpenX Python API client (OAuth authentication, client key and secret)

learn_more.source_type: AWS Identity and Access Management documentation
learn_more.source_title: Security best practices in IAM (update access keys when needed, least privilege)

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed configuration documentation for managing API credentials in the console is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the active API credentials, the integration and owner each belongs to, the scope each carries, and when each was last rotated, to see whether credentials are scoped and rotated rather than shared and left in place.

openx_console.label: OpenX publisher console
steps: Open the API or credentials section of the OpenX console and list every active API credential, such as each consumer key.
For each credential, record the integration it serves, the named owner, and the scope or rights it carries.
Flag any credential shared across more than one person or integration, and any whose scope is broader than its integration needs.
Ask the publisher when each credential was last rotated and whether a rotation schedule and owner exist, and record the answer.
Note any credential that has not been rotated in a long stretch or that has no owner on record.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active API credential, the integration and owner it belongs to, the scope it carries, and when it was last rotated. A credential set where each integration has its own credential, scoped to the rights it needs, with a named owner and a rotation schedule, passes. Credentials shared across people or systems, scoped beyond their integration, or left unrotated with no schedule are a fail recorded with the specific credentials and the gap each represents.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active API credential, its integration, owner, scope, and last rotation above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s OpenX API credentials are shared across people or systems, scoped more broadly than their integrations require, and left without a rotation schedule. A credential that authenticates programmatically can read revenue and change configuration without anyone signing in, so sharing it breaks accountability and leaving it unrotated keeps any leaked copy usable indefinitely. {{voice_recommend}} giving each integration its own credential, scoped to the narrowest rights that task needs, assigning a named owner to each, and setting a rotation cadence. After the change, confirm that every active credential maps to one integration and one owner and that a rotation schedule is in place.
